video_title: ⚡️Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste — @AhmadAwais , CommandCode.ai
gist: Ahmad Awais (CommandCode.ai) argues that the apparent quality gap between open models (DeepSeek V4, Kimi, MiniMax) and frontier closed models (Opus, GPT) is largely a fixable tool-calling/harness problem, not a capability gap. By deterministically repairing malformed tool calls in his coding agent, he claims cheap open models reach competitive performance — a thesis with direct implications for inference-cost economics, the value of model labs vs. harness layers, and DeepSeek's competitive positioning.

themes.theme: Open-model quality gap is a harness/tool-calling artifact

insights.claim: CommandCode.ai's Ahmad Awais claims the perceived weakness of open models like DeepSeek V4 in coding agents is mostly a 'tool confusion' harness problem that is deterministically fixable, not a fundamental model capability gap.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais (guest, CommandCode.ai founder)
insights.so_what: If true, the moat of frontier closed labs in agentic coding is thinner than priced — value migrates from model weights to the harness/tooling layer and to whoever can run cheap open models well.
insights.quote: it can be as silly as something like this... it always ends up being a tool call hardness issue than, you know, an actual model issue

insights.claim: DeepSeek V4 Pro repeats an identical malformed tool call ~56 times on average per billion tokens when sent a Zod schema error, because (per Awais) open models trained on outputs of stronger models learn that 'whatever I am telling you is correct, so don't try to correct me.'
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Suggests distillation-from-stronger-models leaves a systematic failure mode in agentic loops — a quantifiable inference-cost tax that third-party tooling can arbitrage away.
insights.quote: DeepSeek V4 Pro has this weird alpha male energy where whatever it sends you, it thinks that that is the right thing to do

insights.claim: Instead of returning errors, CommandCode deterministically repairs malformed tool-call arguments and returns the result plus a 'repair hint', after which the model's very next (third) tool call is typically corrected and behavior 'becomes super smart'.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: A cheap, deterministic middleware layer can materially upgrade open-model agentic performance — the kind of capability that becomes table stakes and commoditizes a labs-only advantage.
insights.quote: Instead of telling them what to do correctly, you'll first try to save them and then you will explain like why you saved them

insights.claim: The same tool-confusion failure pattern was confirmed across DeepSeek, Kimi, and MiniMax, with CommandCode now maintaining ~16,000 distinct repair variations across hundreds of billions of tokens.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: The defect is systemic across the Chinese open-model cohort, implying a broad, repeatable arbitrage in serving open models — and a data moat for whoever accumulates the repair corpus.
insights.quote: Kimi is doing exactly the same thing. Then we fixed the Kimi models and we fixed MiniMax models and now we have like 16,000 different repair... variations

insights.claim: With repair logic applied, DeepSeek V4 Flash — described as 'practically completely not useful' before — becomes competitive, with one user running 70 billion tokens and 12+ hour sessions on DeepSeek via CommandCode.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Cheap/small open models, once harness-fixed, can sustain long agentic sessions — directly attacking the per-token economics that justify premium closed-model pricing.
insights.quote: this goes from a model that was practically completely not useful like DeepSeek V4 flash to something that can actually compete

insights.claim: Awais claims coding agents perform worse when run with permission prompts on and better with permissions fully bypassed, attributing the degradation to the model being 'steered in the wrong direction' by the slowness/interruptions of permission blocks.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Non-obvious UX/agent-design insight: human-in-the-loop friction may itself degrade model output quality, shaping how autonomous coding products are built.
insights.quote: if you run any coding agent with permissions on the models are actually dumber

themes.theme: Competitive dynamics: harnesses, labs, and DeepSeek pricing

insights.claim: Awais alleges Anthropic's Claude Code hides tool-call errors behind a 'control-O' UI, so users running open models through it see slowness without realizing there are 50+ tool-call failures per session — a friction he says is in Anthropic's 'vested interest' since they didn't build the harness for open models.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Implies frontier labs' first-party harnesses structurally disadvantage rival open models — creating an opening for neutral, open-model-optimized harnesses.
insights.quote: they hide a lot of the errors behind control O... why they don't care about open models. They didn't build their coding agent for open models

insights.claim: Awais asserts Claude is markedly more lenient/robust with malformed tool calls than open models — it can recover from harness errors on its own, which open models cannot — and says this robustness is where CommandCode found product-market fit serving open models.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Tool-call robustness is a real, under-discussed frontier-model differentiator — and the gap is exactly what determines whether cheap open models are usable in production agents.
insights.quote: Claude is actually really really lenient with two calls... Not the case with... open models

insights.claim: Awais speculates that CommandCode's public demonstration of cheap, high-quality DeepSeek inference may have influenced DeepSeek to cut prices to showcase model quality.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Hints at downward price pressure in open-model inference being partly demand-side/perception-driven — relevant to inference-margin compression themes.
insights.quote: I would it wouldn't be too far to say that Deep Seek saw that they can discount their prices

insights.claim: DeepSeek has announced it is hiring to build its own coding agent ('DeepSeek Code'), but Awais expects it will be DeepSeek-only, leaving room for model-agnostic harnesses.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais / host
insights.so_what: Labs moving into the harness layer (vertical integration) is a recurring pattern; single-model lock-in leaves a durable niche for neutral multi-model tools.
insights.quote: they announced that we are hiring for that or whatnot... But, they will only be for Deep Seek

insights.claim: CommandCode launched a $1/month 'Go' plan offering ~600 million DeepSeek tokens to prove open models are catching up, and claims to possibly be the most-used coding agent for DeepSeek inference.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Aggressive token economics signal how cheap open-model serving has become; a credible threat to per-seat/per-token pricing of incumbent coding tools.
insights.quote: we launched a Go Plan with just $1 per month to you can do like 600 million tokens of Deep Seek free for pro in it

insights.claim: Awais reports a model called 'Qwen 3.7 Max' (newly released, '2 or 3 days old') is already the second-most-used model on CommandCode, indicating very fast user migration toward new open/frontier models.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Model loyalty among power users is low and switching is near-instant — pricing power accrues to whoever ships the best frontier model this week, not to brand.
insights.quote: to 3.7 max is the second most used model on Command Code right now. It's just two or three days old

themes.theme: CommandCode scale, funding, and inference volume

insights.claim: CommandCode's lineage (formerly LangBase, an AI cloud) reached 1.2 billion agent runs per month, and the coding agent now processes roughly 600 billion tokens — up ~100x from a few billion tokens/day weeks earlier.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of explosive agentic-coding inference growth — a demand tailwind for inference compute and open-model serving infrastructure.
insights.quote: we are doing anywhere from 600 billion tokens right now

insights.claim: CommandCode raised a $5 million seed round; investors include Tom Preston-Werner's fund (Preston-Werner Ventures) and WordPress creator Matt Mullenweg as an angel.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Signals notable angel/operator interest in open-model-optimized coding tooling as an investable category distinct from the model labs.
insights.quote: when we launched we announced our, you know, $5 million seed

insights.claim: CommandCode plans to fully open-source its 6-year-old coding agent codebase, targeting an announcement at the AI Engineering conference in SF, positioning the product as 'completely hackable.'
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Open-sourcing the harness commoditizes the layer further and pressures closed coding-agent products; the business must monetize elsewhere (taste, hosting, models).
insights.quote: we are going to open source command code very very soon

insights.claim: Awais frames coding-agent strategy via three OS analogies — OpenCode as 'Windows' (every model works), 'Pi/Linux' (build your own drivers), and CommandCode as 'Apple' (curated best open+closed models, not every model, but fully hackable).
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: A curation-over-breadth positioning thesis; useful lens for evaluating which coding-agent businesses can sustain differentiation vs. become commodity routers.
insights.quote: build it like Apple. It will have the best of the best models, both open and closed. It will not have every model, but it will be hackable

insights.claim: CommandCode's 'Taste' is a meta/neuro-symbolic layer that automatically learns a developer's repeated preferences (e.g., pnpm vs npm, Vitest, TS-up) per repository and stores them as transparent, version-controlled markdown 'taste files' reviewed in every PR.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Accumulated, repo-resident preference data is a potential data moat and switching cost — the durable asset when the underlying models are interchangeable.
insights.quote: command code automatically learns from you on a per repository basis... it builds a library of skills

insights.claim: Awais reports a study with 70+ developers showing the number of times they had to manually edit or 're-steer' the LLM dropped when using Taste-learned skill files.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Quantified productivity claim supporting the thesis that learned-context layers, not raw model IQ, drive real-world coding-agent value.
insights.quote: the number of times that they had to go edit files... They found that their number of edits or steers went down

insights.claim: An emerging power-user pattern in CommandCode's community: build/seed a project with an expensive high-quality model (Opus, GPT 5.5) to generate a taste file, then use very cheap models to continue building against that taste file.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: A 'distill expensive taste, execute cheap' workflow that structurally reduces demand for premium-model tokens — bearish for closed-model token volume per task.
insights.quote: they're building one project with a really high quality LLM like Opus or GPT 5.5... and then they're using... super cheap models to continuously build on that

insights.claim: CommandCode says programmers have largely stopped writing code themselves, so it now learns 'taste' from the models' outputs rather than from humans — a stated shift in where coding signal originates.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Reflects how fast human-authored code is being displaced in agentic workflows — relevant to developer-tooling TAM and where future training/feedback data comes from.
insights.quote: programmers stopped writing code. So, who are we going to learn taste from... we started learning taste from models because models are writing code

themes.theme: Generalizing the repair pattern: design and security

insights.claim: Awais claims ~90% of LLM 'design slop' (e.g., indigo-purple gradients) is fixable not by better models but by a 'contract gap' fix: ~24 reference docs, 10 design 'smells', and 7 surface patterns derived from interviewing designers.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Same 'deterministic repair beats bigger model' thesis extended to design — reinforces that productized taste/contracts, not raw model scale, capture value.
insights.quote: it's like a feels like you can fix 90% of design slop, which is not a capability gap. It's more like a contract gap

insights.claim: LLMs control color far better when forced to use the OKLCH color space than HSL, so CommandCode's design skill forces OKLCH — a concrete, transferable prompt-engineering lever.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Granular, reproducible technique that any harness can adopt — illustrates how cheap deterministic constraints close perceived quality gaps.
insights.quote: if you force an LLM to use OKLCH, they can control the colors palette really really well compared to any of other things

insights.claim: CommandCode is extending the deterministic-repair approach to security, with a team member working with a security community to auto-detect the most common insecure-code patterns and apply automatic fixes.
insights.speaker: Ahmad Awais
insights.so_what: Signals the repair-pattern playbook generalizing into a platform (code, design, security) — a broader product/TAM thesis if it lands.
insights.quote: the same problem can be applied to security as well... they can apply automatic fixes to your packages
